questions.prompt: You have a table orders(order_id, customer_id, order_date, total_amount). Write a query to list customer_id and total_amount for orders placed on '2024-01-15'. Which of the following SELECT statements returns only those two columns correctly?
options: SELECT customer_id, total_amount FROM orders WHERE order_date = '2024-01-15';
SELECT customer_id total_amount FROM orders WHERE order_date = '2024-01-15';
SELECT customer_id, total_amount, order_date FROM orders WHERE order_date = '2024-01-15';
questions.expected: SELECT customer_id, total_amount FROM orders WHERE order_date = '2024-01-15';

questions.prompt: Given a products table(product_id, name, price, discontinued) — to find active products priced under 50, you write: SELECT * FROM products WHERE price < 50 AND discontinued = false; True or False: This WHERE clause will correctly filter out discontinued products and return only price < 50 rows.

turns.label: Turn 2
turns.briefing: Complete Turn 2. Use the reference items on the desk.

questions.prompt: Now we extend the case: there is a sales table(sale_id, product_id, sale_date, quantity, sale_amount). Write a single SQL query that returns product_id and total_quantity_sold for sales in January 2024, grouping by product_id. Use the table and date range in your answer.
questions.expected: SELECT product_id, SUM(quantity) AS total_quantity_sold FROM sales WHERE sale_date BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-01-31' GROUP BY product_id;
questions.idealAnswer: SELECT product_id, SUM(quantity) AS total_quantity_sold FROM sales WHERE sale_date BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-01-31' GROUP BY product_id;

questions.prompt: You need to combine customers(customer_id, name) with orders(order_id, customer_id, total_amount) to list all customers and any matching order totals. Which JOIN type should you use so every customer appears even if they have no orders?

You must filter users who have made at least one order. Which approach is more efficient for most databases: using EXISTS with a correlated subquery or using an INNER JOIN to orders and DISTINCT on users? Choose the best option for performance and explainability in the interface.
options: INNER JOIN users u JOIN orders o ON u.id = o.user_id with DISTINCT
EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM orders o WHERE o.user_id = u.id)
LEFT JOIN and WHERE o.user_id IS NOT NULL
Use UNION of users and orders
questions.expected: EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM orders o WHERE o.user_id = u.id)

turns.label: Turn 3
turns.briefing: Complete Turn 3. Use the reference items on the desk.

questions.prompt: Your reporting requirement now asks for each salesperson (salesperson_id) the running total of sale_amount ordered by sale_date within each salesperson for 2024. Provide the SQL using window functions on sales(sale_id, salesperson_id, sale_date, sale_amount).
questions.expected: SELECT salesperson_id, sale_date, sale_amount, SUM(sale_amount) OVER (PARTITION BY salesperson_id ORDER BY sale_date ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) AS running_total FROM sales WHERE sale_date BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-12-31';
questions.idealAnswer: SELECT salesperson_id, sale_date, sale_amount, SUM(sale_amount) OVER (PARTITION BY salesperson_id ORDER BY sale_date ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) AS running_total FROM sales WHERE sale_date BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-12-31';

questions.prompt: You are designing a transactions table that will be queried frequently by account_id and timestamp. Describe a normalization and indexing strategy that balances write performance and read performance for a high-traffic OLTP system. Include specific index types and whether to use single table or vertical partitioning. Use the case context (queries by account_id and time ranges).
questions.idealAnswer: Normalize to avoid duplicating account metadata; keep transactions tall (one row per transaction). Use a clustered index on (account_id, timestamp) for efficient range scans per account, and a non-clustered covering index for common filters (status, amount). Consider horizontal partitioning by time ranges to prune old partitions; this speeds reads and simplifies archiving but adds complexity for writes and cross-partition queries.

questions.prompt: A particular query joining orders and order_items is slow: SELECT o.order_id, o.order_date, SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) FROM orders o JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id WHERE o.order_date >= '2024-01-01' GROUP BY o.order_id, o.order_date; Diagnose likely causes and propose three concrete optimizations. Base your answer on the query and table join keys.
questions.idealAnswer: Possible causes: missing index on order_items.order_id causing large nested-loop scans; lack of index on orders.order_date preventing date range seek; outdated statistics. Optimizations: add an index on order_items(order_id) or a composite index (order_id, unit_price, quantity) to make the aggregation from the index; add an index on orders(order_date) to limit rows; ensure statistics are up-to-date and consider partitioning orders by date for large tables.

questions.prompt: You're preparing a migration: order history older than 3 years will be archived. Order the migration steps for minimal downtime: A) Create archive table with same schema; B) Add indexes to archive table; C) Copy old rows in batches to archive; D) Verify row counts and constraints; E) Drop old rows from production; F) Back up production database. Provide the correct operational sequence.

You have a table orders(order_id, customer_id, order_date, total_amount). Write a query to list customer_id and total_amount for orders placed on '2024-01-15'. Which of the following SELECT statements returns only those two columns correctly?

Given a products table(product_id, name, price, discontinued) — to find active products priced under 50, you write: SELECT * FROM products WHERE price < 50 AND discontinued = false; True or False: This WHERE clause will correctly filter out discontinued products and return only price < 50 rows.

Now we extend the case: there is a sales table(sale_id, product_id, sale_date, quantity, sale_amount). Write a single SQL query that returns product_id and total_quantity_sold for sales in January 2024, grouping by product_id. Use the table and date range in your answer.
questions.idealAnswer: SELECT product_id, SUM(quantity) AS total_quantity_sold FROM sales WHERE sale_date BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-01-31' GROUP BY product_id;

questions.prompt: You need to combine customers(customer_id, name) with orders(order_id, customer_id, total_amount) to list all customers and any matching order totals. Which JOIN type should you use so every customer appears even if they have no orders?

You must filter users who have made at least one order. Which approach is more efficient for most databases: using EXISTS with a correlated subquery or using an INNER JOIN to orders and DISTINCT on users? Choose the best option for performance and explainability in the interface.
questions.answer: EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM orders o WHERE o.user_id = u.id)

questions.prompt: Your reporting requirement now asks for each salesperson (salesperson_id) the running total of sale_amount ordered by sale_date within each salesperson for 2024. Provide the SQL using window functions on sales(sale_id, salesperson_id, sale_date, sale_amount).
questions.idealAnswer: SELECT salesperson_id, sale_date, sale_amount, SUM(sale_amount) OVER (PARTITION BY salesperson_id ORDER BY sale_date ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) AS running_total FROM sales WHERE sale_date BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-12-31';
